summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – ADOPTION – Leave to commence proceedings – Where the Applicants seek parenting orders and leave to commence adoption proceedings in the County Court of Victoria pursuant to s 60G(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) – Where the Respondent biological father did not participate in the proceedings – Orders as sought by the Applicants made.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARTNETT J

PRELIMINARY

The Court made final orders in this matter on 23 March 2026. What follows are the reasons for the making of those orders.
Before the Court was an application for parenting orders and an application for the Second Applicant stepfather (“the stepfather”), with the support of the First Applicant mother (“the mother”) (collectively “the applicants”) to have leave to commence proceedings in the County Court of Victoria for the adoption of the child, the subject of the proceeding, X born in 2016 (“[X]”). X is now aged 10 years.
The respondent biological father of X (“the respondent”) did not file any material, nor did he participate in the proceeding or in the pre-trial procedures into which the applicants put considerable resources in their efforts to engage the father. The father ultimately did not make himself available for a mediation in which he would have been legally represented. I am satisfied that he was served with the Initiating Application for Final Orders together with all material as relied upon by the applicants and all orders in the proceeding as made by the Court.
The applicants filed an Initiating Application to commence proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 5 November 2025. The proceeding was transferred to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) pursuant to s 149 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) (“the FCFCOA Act”) on 18 December 2025.

ORDERS SOUGHT

The applicants sought:
(1) leave to proceed undefended;

(2) parenting orders that:

(a) discharge the previous final parenting orders made 7 February 2022;

(b) provide the applicants with joint decision-making responsibility for X and for her to live with them; and

(c) permit the registration of a change of X’s surname from Confortola to Shyam; and

(3) leave pursuant to s 60G of the Act to commence proceedings to adopt X.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The applicants relied upon:
(1) their Initiating Application filed 5 November 2025;

(2) the mother’s affidavit filed 5 November 2025, together with annexures;

(3) the stepfathers’ affidavit filed 5 November 2025, together with annexures;

(4) the Victoria Police s 67ZBD response dated 16 December 2025 (now marked Exhibit A-1);

(5) the affidavits of service of Ms C, filed by the applicants on 26 February 2026, 22 December 2025 and 8 December 2025; and of Ms B, filed by the applicants on 8 December 2025 (collectively “the affidavits of service”). The affidavits of service established the service (by substituted service as ordered by the Court) upon the respondent of the Initiating Application; the affidavits and material relied upon by the applicants; and the orders as made throughout the proceeding; and

(6) the written submissions of counsel.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Parenting orders

Parental responsibility is dealt with in Division 2 of Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). Relevantly, s 61CA of the Act provides as follows:
Consultation between parents on major long - term issues

If it is safe to do so, and subject to any court orders, the parents of a child who is not yet 18 are encouraged:
(a) to consult each other about major long-term issues in relation to the child; and

(b) in doing so, to have regard to the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.

(Emphasis in original)
Relevantly, s 61D of the Act provides as follows:
Parenting orders and parental responsibility
(1) A parenting order confers parental responsibility for a child on a person, but only to the extent to which the order confers on the person duties, powers, responsibilities or authority in relation to the child.

(2) A parenting order in relation to a child does not take away or diminish any aspect of the parental responsibility of any person for the child except to the extent (if any):

(a) expressly provided for in the order; or

(b) necessary to give effect to the order.
(3) A parenting order that deals with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions about major long - term issues in relation to the child (see subsection 64B(3)) may provide for joint or sole decision - making in relation to all or specified major long - term issues.
(Emphasis in original)
Pursuant to s 60CA of the Act:
In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to a child, a court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.
To determine the best interests of the child, I must have regard to the considerations in the Act as set out in ss 60CC(1), 60CC (2) and 60CC(2A) of the Act. Those sections provide, relevantly:
How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests

...

(1) ...in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2);
General considerations
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;
(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

(Emphasis in original)
When considering the risk of family violence, the following is set out in s 60CG of the Act:
(1) In considering what order to make, the court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child’s best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order:
(a) is consistent with any family violence order; and

(b) does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the court may include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.
In considering these matters, the Court must give consideration as to whether there is an unacceptable risk of family violence and/or an inability to promote the safety of a child and parent in the making of parenting orders also involving a consideration of unacceptable risk. Upon establishing the existence of an unacceptable risk, the Court must then determine whether that risk “is able to be sufficiently managed or ameliorated”.[1]
The Court is required to assess its factual findings on the balance of probabilities, and the impact of those findings upon the assessment of future risk. Accordingly, Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) 65 Fam LR 288 is applicable. The Court’s determination as to what did or did not happen in the past is determined on the balance of probabilities. However, in hypothesising about future possibilities the Court is required to engage in an assessment of risk as a predictive exercise.[2] That assessment of risk is informed by the factual findings as to the past.
Pursuant to s 65D of the Act, the Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper, including by discharging earlier parenting orders. In cases where there have been final parenting orders made and in force, s 65DAAA(1) provides that the Court must not reconsider these orders unless satisfied that there has been a significant change of circumstances and that in all the circumstances it is in the best interests of the child for the parenting order to be reconsidered.

Leave to commence adoption proceedings

Adoption proceedings in Victoria are determined by application of the relevant State legislation, being the Adoption Act 1984 (Vic) (“the Adoption Act”). Notwithstanding this, there is provision in the Act for the Court to provide leave to commence adoption proceedings in other courts, and in this instance the County Court of Victoria (“the County Court”).
Section 60G of the Act relevantly provides:
(1) Subject to subsection (2), the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), may grant leave for proceedings to be commenced for the adoption of a child by a prescribed adopting parent.

(2) In proceedings for leave under subsection (1), the court must consider whether granting leave would be in the child’s best interests, having regard to the effect of paragraph 60F(4)(a), or paragraph 60HA(3)(a), and of sections 61E and 65J.

Relevant to s 60G (1) of the Act, the meaning of a prescribed adopting parent, in relation to a child, is included in s 4(1) of the Act which provides:
(a) a parent of the child; or

(b) the spouse of, or a person in a de facto relationship with, a parent of the child; or

(c) a parent of the child and either his or her spouse or a person in a de facto relationship with the parent.

Relevant to s 60G (2) of the Act, the “best interests” considerations the Court must have regard to, are as set out in s 60CC of the Act.
Section s 60F(4)(a), relevantly, of the Act makes provision in relation to the child of a marriage who is adopted by the prescribed adopting parent:
if a court granted leave under section 60G for the adoption proceedings to be commenced—the child ceases to be a child of the marriage for the purposes of this Act.
Pursuant to s 61E of the Act, the effect of adoption on parental responsibility is, relevantly, if:
(1) ...
(a) a child is adopted; and

(b) immediately before the adoption, a person had parental responsibility for the child, whether in full or to a limited extent and whether because of section 61C or because of a parenting order.

(2) The person’s parental responsibility for the child ends on the adoption of the child, unless the adoption is by a prescribed adopting parent and leave was not granted under section 60G for the adoption proceedings to be commenced.

Like s 61E of the Act, s 65J of the Act provides the effect of adoption on a parenting order is relevantly if:
(1) ...
(a) a child is adopted; and

(b) immediately before the adoption, a parenting order was in force in relation to the child.

(2) The parenting order stops being in force on the adoption of the child, unless the adoption is by a prescribed adopting parent and leave was not granted under section 60G for the adoption proceedings to be commenced.

Having regard to ss 61E and 65J of the Act, I note that the definition of prescribed adopting parent in s 4(1) of the Act includes provision for a parent of the child being an applicant, in addition to the spouse of a parent. These are the factual circumstances in this matter.
It is not mandatory under the Adoption Act for leave to be granted by this Court before an adoption order can be applied for in the County Court of Victoria. However, in this proceeding, the applicants seek both parenting orders and leave to apply for adoption orders in the County Court, a matter further elaborated upon hereafter.

RELEVANT FACTUAL FINDINGS

The factual background to this proceeding is set out in the applicants’ affidavits. The applicants’ evidence was unchallenged (the matter proceeding undefended) and is accepted by the Court.
The mother is 37 years of age and was born on in 1988. Her partner, the stepfather, is 54 years of age and was born in 1971.
X’s biological father, the respondent, is 40 years of age and was born in 1986.
The mother married the respondent in 2014. The only child of their marriage, X, was born in 2016.
Around the time of X’s birth, the mother became aware that the respondent was increasingly using drugs and gambling online with their savings. Around this time, the respondent also became unemployed. He had been a tradesperson and lost that job. He then became a tradesperson as an independent contractor. But that job also did not last. The mother and the respondent were living in a rental property but struggled to make the rental payments given the mother had ceased work to look after X.
In 2016, the mother’s brother passed away and her mother (“the maternal grandmother”) and two sisters travelled to Country D for a three-month grieving period. During this time, the mother, the respondent and X moved from their rental accommodation into the maternal grandmother’s home. It is the mother’s evidence that she felt like a single parent; was solely responsible for X’s care; and frequently argued with the respondent about his drug use and gambling. The marriage failed and the mother and respondent separated under the one roof in August 2016.
The mother returned to work when X was an infant. The maternal grandmother cared for X while the mother was at work, and the respondent did not assist in the provision of that care. The respondent was using drugs to the extent that he struggled to maintain a routine, or function in everyday life. He would go out each evening as the mother returned home from work and slept during the day.
In or around early 2017, the respondent received threats following a drug deal, including that people would come to the family’s home. The respondent’s brother lent him a substantial sum of money to pay for the drug debt owing by the respondent.
In or around March 2017, the mother asked the respondent to leave her mother’s family home as she felt his behaviour posed a risk to X and her mother, sisters and herself. family. The respondent left the home. The mother and respondent were divorced in October 2018.
In early 2019, X was three years of age. She saw the respondent in that year on X’s birthday and has not seen him since. That is a period of seven years.
X’s lack of contact with the respondent was entirely of the respondent’s making. The mother attempted to organise regular time between X and the respondent following the separation of she and the respondent, but all her attempts failed because of a lack of commitment by the respondent. The mother described months passing at times, without contact from the respondent. When time did occur, issues arose, two as described hereafter:[3]
On one occasion, I invited [Mr Confortola] to attend a [concert] with [X] and myself when she was around 2 ½ years old. I thought that I would try a different approach to co-parenting and spend time together with [Mr Confortola] and [X] so that she could spend time with him and I was there if anything went wrong. [Mr Confortola] presented as happy, bubbly and engaged. In retrospect I believe [Mr Confortola] may have been drug affected at the time. The occasion went well until after [Mr Confortola] dropped us home. After I had returned home and had put [X] to sleep, [Mr Confortola] called me and went on a rant for a very long time about matters that were of no real importance, about issues he said had arisen between my friend’s husband and himself and blaming them for the bad things in his life. I found this unusual. [Mr Confortola] then suddenly ended the call and shortly after my mother arrived home and as she came in the front door, she asked me what [Mr Confortola] was doing in his car outside the house. I did not realise [Mr Confortola] was still outside the house. A couple of hours later [Mr Confortola] called my cousin [Mr E]. [Mr E] told me and I verily believe that during the call [Mr Confortola] was ranting and raving, saying that he wanted to kill himself and that he was on the side of a freeway. [Mr E] went and picked [Mr Confortola] up, calling me to tell me that [Mr Confortola] was shaking and screaming in rage that he wanted to see me. [Mr E] drove [Mr Confortola] to my house, but I refused to see [Mr Confortola] as his behaviour was aggressive and out of control. [Mr E] realised that [Mr Confortola] was coming down off some drug use.

[...]

Prior to [X’s] third birthday [in 2019] [X] had not seen [Mr Confortola] for months. I organised for her to spend time with [Mr Confortola] with his brother supervising and I understood that they were going to visit [Mr Confortola’s] father’s home as his father was leaving for an overseas holiday. I expected [Mr Confortola’s] family to celebrate [X’s] birthday as they had not celebrated one with her previously. After only 2 hours [Mr Confortola] called me and arranged to drop [X] back to me. [X] has not seen [Mr Confortola] since that occasion.
In December 2018, the applicants began their relationship. They commenced cohabitation in August 2019.
In August 2019 the mother and the respondent engaged in mediation and the mother was provided with a s 60I certificate. The mother has not subsequently seen the respondent. The stepfather has never had any direct contact with the respondent.
Following the mediation, the mother and the maternal grandmother received harassing text messages from the respondent’s sister, Ms F. The mother applied for and obtained an intervention order against Ms F in October 2019 that operated for two years, naming herself and X as protected persons.
The mother supported the paternal grandfather to see X in around 2020 and indicated to the paternal grandfather that he was welcome in X’s life.
X has however had no contact with any member of the respondent’s family since 2020.
In or around 2020, when aged four years, X was looking through photographs and did not initially recognise the respondent, confusing him with the mother’s brother who had passed away. Around that time, X began to ask questions about why she had a different surname to the mother. The mother took X to a creative play psychologist to support her to understand the respondent’s role in her life and changes to her family. X saw the psychologist until 2024.
X is aware that she has a biological father and has demonstrated that she recollects calling someone “dad”. X is also aware that the stepfather is not her biological father and has an age-appropriate understanding of her family relationships. The applicants do not discourage X from asking questions or speaking about, the respondent.
In or around February 2021, a friend advised the mother that while shopping in Suburb G she saw the respondent among a group of homeless people.
In or around April 2021, another friend advised the mother that her partner had spoken with the respondent in the city, and been told that the respondent was homeless and living on the street. Also in or around April 2021, the friend who had seen the respondent in Suburb G advised the mother that she had spoken with the respondent’s brother who said that he had begged the respondent to attend rehabilitation and told him he would support him financially to get clean, but the respondent refused.
The respondent, in the circumstances, did not and does not contribute financially to the support of X and nor does he provide for any of the physical, emotional, cultural or other needs of X. The applicants together provide for all X’s needs as referred to hereafter.
On 3 September 2021, the mother filed an Initiating Application seeking parenting orders in relation to X (proceedings MLC9833/2021). The respondent was the respondent to those proceedings.
The mother did not know the respondent’s then whereabouts and made various attempts to contact him. An order for substituted service was made on 8 November 2021.
On 7 February 2022 final parenting orders were made in the proceedings between the mother and the respondent. The mother was given leave to proceed on an undefended basis. The orders provided for the mother to have sole parental responsibility for X, for X to live with the mother and for the father to spend time with X as agreed between the parties in writing.
The applicants married in late 2022. There are two children of the applicants’ marriage:
(1) H, born 2023 (“[H]”); and

(2) J, born 2025 (“[J]”).

The stepfather also has three adult children from his previous marriage, namely, Ms K (aged 24), Ms L (aged 21) and Mr N (aged 19).

The respondent’s criminal history

Before moving to the stepfather’s relationship with X, it is important to record the material as set out below which corroborates (and expands upon) the mother’s evidence as to the respondent’s use of drugs and its adverse impact upon his behaviours which has included his inability to parent X.
The Victoria Police s 67ZBD response dated 16 December 2025 (Exhibit A-1) outlined the respondent’s extensive criminal history in recent years, including the following:
(1) In early 2021, the Bail and Remand Court convicted and sentenced the respondent to a 12-month Community Corrections Order for possessing an illicit substance, committing an indictable offence whilst on bail, criminal damage (intent to damage/destroy), burglary and possessing a prohibited weapon.

(2) In mid-2022, the Melbourne Magistrates’ Court sentenced the respondent to imprisonment reckoned as already served (as he had spent 69 days in custody) and an 18-month Community Corrections Order for a range of offences, including unlicensed driving, unlawful assault, possessing a drug of dependence (not named), contravening the Community Corrections Order, possessing an illicit substance, committing an indictable offence whilst on bail, possessing a prohibited weapon, criminal damage (intent to damage/destroy) and burglary.

(3) In late 2024, the Melbourne County Court convicted and sentenced the respondent to 11 months imprisonment and an 18-month Community Corrections Order upon completion of the term in relation to an aggravated burglary (with a person present).

(4) In late 2024, the Broadmeadows Magistrates’ Court varied his 18-month Community Corrections Order upon release from imprisonment in relation to convictions for contravening a Community Corrections Order, possessing a drug of dependence (not named), unlicensed driving and unlawful assault.

It is noted in the report that in relation to an incident in late 2022, the respondent was reportedly in possession of a handgun when he broke into a home with another person and demanded money from the homeowner.

The applicant stepfather

The stepfather met X in early 2019 after his relationship with the mother commenced. At the time, she had turned three. The stepfather’s evidence is that he shares a bond with X that is natural and deeply meaningful. He deposed to being respectful of the fact that she has a biological father and being careful to ensure she understood in an age-appropriate way that he is not her biological father.
The stepfather is actively involved in all aspects of parenting X, including through attending school events and extra-curricular activities, dropping her off to school each morning, supporting her creative side and providing emotional, physical and financial support. He reported that X spontaneously began referring to him as “dad”, reflecting her experience of their relationship.
X is close with her half-siblings and step-siblings. They have an engaged and loving relationship. The mother has described X as thriving, beautiful, funny, sporty, eccentric and full of creativity and warmth. The stepfather has described her as bright and loving.
The children H and J have different surnames to X. X has said that she would like the surname Shyam, and the applicants intend to change all their surnames (including the mother’s) to Shyam once X is able to change her surname.
The issues experienced by the applicants in relation to X’s surname, and the stepfather’s lack of decision-making responsibility, include in relation to communication with her school, providing medical consent, receiving documentation and while travelling as a family.
It is the applicants’ evidence that they were motivated to commence this proceeding because of the closeness of their integrated family unit. X has known the stepfather for most of her life. The mother and stepfather share decisions about the children’s lives in relation to schooling and allied health. Both support X’s attendance at M School where she is in Grade 5.

CONSIDERATION

Pursuant to s 65C of the Act, the applicants both have standing to apply for a parenting order, as X’s mother and stepfather concerned with her care, welfare and development.

Leave to proceed undefended

Throughout this proceeding, the respondent failed to comply with Court orders as to his placing of evidence before the Court and sought no orders. He did not challenge the evidence of the applicants in any way. The respondent also failed to appear at any hearing or provide any explanation as to his absence at any hearing. He was properly served and given the opportunity to participate. In essence, he failed entirely to engage in the proceeding.
In these circumstances, the applicants were granted leave to proceed on an undefended basis under rules 1.33(2)(c) and 15.19(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth). I am satisfied that pursuant to s 102NE of the Act, it was in X’s best interests for the proceeding to be conducted without undue delay.

Parenting orders

Pursuant to s 60CA of the Act, the best interests of the child are the paramount consideration when making parenting orders.
In relation to s 60CC(2)(a) and the promotion of X’s safety, I accept that the respondent has a background of drug addiction, gambling and problem behaviour as detailed in the relevant factual findings above. His criminal history (Exhibit A-1) is evidence of the fact that his issues with substance use and criminal offending have continued in recent years. I also accept the mother’s unchallenged evidence in relation to the respondent’s behaviours toward her.
Throughout her life, X has benefitted from her mother’s care, as reflected in the previous final parenting orders made 7 February 2022. She has further benefitted from the introduction of the stepfather into her life, culminating in changes to her family relationships through the applicants’ marriage and the birth of her siblings. I am satisfied that the stepfather’s contributions to X’s care and welfare constitute a change in circumstances.
I accept the applicants’ evidence in relation to X’s own views in relation to s 60CC(2)(b), including that she has been supported by a psychologist to understand changes to her family; has an age-appropriate understanding of her family relationships; wants to change her surname; and spontaneously began viewing the stepfather as a father figure and calling him “dad”.
In relation to ss 60CC(2)(c)-(d), X’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs are met by the applicants, and they have capacity to continue to meet these needs. The parenting orders they seek reflect the realities of X’s life, where she is well cared for and nurtured by both applicants and bonded to her siblings.
In relation to s 60CC(2)(e), the respondent has had a lack of involvement in parenting X and did not engage in this proceeding. X has close relationships with her mother, stepfather and siblings who constitute her family unit.
In relation to s 60CC(2)(f), I accept that orders providing parental responsibility to the stepfather and mother would facilitate the stepfather’s ability to fulfil his parenting role in X’s day-to-day life.
I am satisfied that the parenting orders sought by the applicants are in X’s best interests. The submissions as prepared by counsel for the applicants in this regard were comprehensive and assisted the Court in the expression of that satisfaction.

Leave to commence adoption proceedings

The discretion of the Court to grant leave for adoption proceedings to be commenced provided by s 60G(1) of the Act is enlivened when the application is made by a prescribed adopting parent.
As the mother is X’s parent and the stepfather is her spouse, the applicants are each a prescribed adopting parent for the purposes of the subsection.
As the mother and respondent were previously married, I must consider s 60F(4)(a) of the Act which provides that a child who would otherwise be considered a child of a marriage as prescribed by s 60F(1)(a), is no longer a child of that marriage where leave under s 60G is granted in respect of that child.
In this case, the presumption that each of the child’s parents has parental responsibility was displaced by the final parenting orders made 7 February 2022. Given the evidence of the role that each of the mother, stepfather and respondent have provided and will continue to provide to X, I accept that her welfare will be supported and not prejudiced by the grant of leave with the effect that she will therefore cease to be a child of the marriage between the mother and the respondent.
The effect of s 61E of the Act is that upon the adoption of a child, the parental responsibility for that child (by reason of a parenting order or otherwise) vested in any person, ends (except in circumstances which are not presently relevant).
Similarly, s 65J of the Act provides that upon the adoption of a child any parenting order in force immediately before the adoption stops being force (again except in circumstances which are not presently relevant).
Section 65J poses no issue in the present case as the extant orders proscribe no time with or communication between X and the respondent other than what might be agreed by the mother. Any such communication ceased by August 2019, and the respondent not only failed to pursue any such opportunities but has elected not to participate in this proceeding.
Section 61E, on its face, seems to suggest that upon a successful adoption of X by the stepfather, both the mother and the respondent will cease to have parental responsibility.
His Honour Justice Gill considered the perceived difficulty with s 61E in Hanlon & Hanlon [2022] FedCFamC1F 435, in relation to adoption proceedings in the Australian Capital Territory in the following extract from his judgment:
A plain reading of the FLA provisions would lead to the conclusion that, pursuant to s 61E, in a general case the parental responsibility of both parents would be extinguished, which in this case means that the parental responsibility of Ms Hanlon [the biological mother of the child X] would be removed, despite Ms Hanlon remaining in a parental relationship with X. That is, while Ms Hanlon retains her status as X’s parent by operation of the Territory law, by operation of the Act it is a status that is stripped of parental responsibility. At the same time the current orders that provide for the sharing of parental responsibility between Ms Hanlon and Mr Hanlon would also dissolve by operation of s 65J of the FLA.
The effect of such a construction would be that Mr Hanlon, as a parent by virtue of the adoption pursuant to the Territory Act, would, by operation of s 61C of the FLA, have parental responsibility whilst Ms Hanlon, who remains a parent, would not, subject to a further parenting order being made. This is a somewhat perverse result.
This was, however, the outcome identified in a number of first instance decisions, including by Strickland J in Brock & Brock [2007] FamCA 1594 where, in relation to a step-parent adoption he said at [12]:
The effect of Section 61E I have addressed in some detail with Mr Frost and that led to the application to amend the orders sought. To repeat though, the effect of Section 61E is that, if I grant leave and the adoption order is made, the parental responsibility of any person who had parental responsibility for K immediately before the adoption ceases. That applies specifically to Mrs Brock who is the mother of K.
Similar concerns were expressed by O’Reilly J in Tanner & Cape [2011] FamCA 665, where her Honour, expressing concern at such an effect, nonetheless dealt with the application on the basis that such an outcome may flow at [15]:
If s 61E be given its literal meaning, unless Mrs Tanner the biological mother is a co-applicant for adoption she would lose her parental responsibility upon an adoption in favour of Mr Tanner the child’s step father. Probably s 61E should be read down to exclude the biological parent who is the spouse or partner of the adopting step parent (if not amended to that effect) as it is an awkward proposition to think that one could apply to adopt one’s own child and indeed that is not the effect of the State Act, Part 5, Div 1, s 92. Other judges however have taken the view that the order should be made in favour of both the step parent and the biological parent who is the spouse or partner of the step parent on the basis that otherwise upon the making of an adoption order in favour of a step father the biological mother’s parental responsibility for the child would end. See, for example, Bacon & Bacon [2007] FamCA 1594 at [2] and [3] per Strickland J; Rohrlach, Rohrlach & Doddridge [2008] FamCA 518 at [2] per Strickland J; and as to the effect of s 61E: Beck & Major & Wright [2010] FamCA 75 at [12] per Murphy J. Although thus I doubt that the mother can apply to adopt her own child, the existence of s 61E in its present form dictates that as a precaution I include her in the order, as indeed was sought in the application.
Hence it can be seen that a plain reading of the legislation has been, reasonably, taken to have the effect of removing parental responsibility in the manner identified above.
Respectfully, however, such a construction is incorrect.
Construing s 61E as stripping parental responsibility from Ms Hanlon is, at face value, a perverse or unreasonable outcome. It conflicts with the apparent purpose of the leave provisions to regulate the interaction of Commonwealth and Territory law in the case of a step-parent adoption and to protect the operation of the FLA from being undermined, as it contrasts with the explicit operation of the Territory law that it interacts with that preserves her parental status untouched by the adoption.
Given what is at face value an unreasonable result, it is open to make reference to the Explanatory Memorandum, as s 15AB of the Acts Interpretation Act 1901 (Cth) permits reference to such extrinsic material in the case of apparent unreasonableness. It is this Explanatory Memorandum that is of most assistance in determining the underlying statutory intent of the provisions as directed toward the ending of the parental responsibility of one parent on adoption by a step-parent, being that parent who is not partnered with the step-parent. Twice in that Memorandum to the Family Law Reform Act 1995 (Cth) there is reference to “the other parent.” It appears that the legislature was contemplating the stripping of parental responsibility from the “other parent” rather than from the parent partner of the applicant step-parent.
Such an understanding is rather more consistent with the operation of s 60F of the FLA which deals with the identification of X as a child of a particular marriage. The effect here is not to remove Ms Hanlon from the description on adoption by Mr Hanlon, but rather to mean simply that X is no longer a child of the marriage between Ms Hanlon and Mr B, but a child of the marriage between Ms Hanlon and Mr Hanlon, a result that specifically preserves the connection of X with respect to Ms Hanlon.
The more brutalist literal understanding also sits uncomfortably with the implicit purpose of the provisions to prevent conflict between State and Territory adoption orders and Federal orders regarding parental responsibility, in that it effectively extends the effect and impact of the adoption order beyond that contemplated by the Territory Act, to impact on the parental responsibility of the partner parent. This, explicitly, is not contemplated under the Territory legislation.
The absurdity of an interpretation that removes Ms Hanlon’s parental responsibility for X may also be observed in that the adoption is by Mr Hanlon, who is a prescribed adopting parent by virtue of his relationship with Ms Hanlon. His relationship with X, and hence his application in relation to adoption is inextricably connected to Ms Hanlon, her relationship with X, and her status as X’s parent.
The unreasonable nature of these aspects of such a literal interpretation point toward the need, in order to capture the legislative intent, for s 61E to, in a manner consistent with Project Blue Sky, “be read in a way that does not correspond with the literal or grammatical meaning”. In this instance that requires an understanding that the mischiefs addressed by the provisions relate to potential inconsistency between a Territory adoption order and extant parental responsibility under the Act, and to the prevention of the undermining of the Act in allocation of parental responsibility by use of the Territory adoption legislation. Such an understanding requires a reading of s 61E(1)(b) such that the reference to “parental responsibility” is taken to be restricted to parental responsibility that is inconsistent with the adoption order.
Such an effect harmonises s 61E with the operation of both s 60F and s 60HA, which reflects the scope of effect of the adoption order that the Act interacts with and prevents a perverse result. It is consistent with the requirement for interpretation in context as described in Statutory Interpretation in Australia 9th Ed – Dennis Pearce, where the learned author identifies what was authoritatively stated by Edelman J in SAS Trustee [2018] HCA 55; (2018) 265 CLR 137, at [64] where his Honour described:
... [T]his Court said that the task of statutory construction must begin and end with the text of the statute. That statement does not mean that the text of a statute must be interpreted only according to the range of semantic meanings of the individual words. It means only that the interpretation of a statute, like any other legal instrument, is an interpretation of its words. Those words are interpreted in their context and in light of their purpose although legal rules can sometimes exclude or restrict the use of some context. In ascertaining the reasonably intended meaning of Parliament context is, literally, those matters to be considered (simultaneously) together with the text. Context can give words an interpretation that is the opposite of their ordinary meaning and grammatical sense. Context can also permit a construction of words that excludes their application to matters that would have fallen within the application of their literal meaning. However, as with contractual interpretation, where “the clearer the natural meaning the more difficult it is to justify departing from it”, so too in statutory interpretation “questions of degree arise and it will be more difficult to displace an interpretation that “has a powerful advantage in ordinary meaning and grammatical sense”.
(Citations omitted)
In summary here, a reading of s 61E that is not restrictive in the sense described above, so conflicts with the statutory context of the provision as to fail to reflect the legislative intent in the enactment. The brutalist literal meaning of the provision if taken at face value conflicts with the notion that the legislature was pursuing its purposes “by coherent means” as described by Gageler J in SAS Trustee. Such an understanding ought also be applied to the reading of the definition of “parent” at s 4 of the Act. Again it should be construed, consistently with the above reasoning, as referring to the adoptive parent as opposed to the displaced parent.
The consequence of these conclusions is that no buttressing order is required to restore the parental responsibility of Ms Hanlon on the making of an adoption order in favour of Mr Hanlon, as Ms Hanlon and Mr Hanlon will, on adoption of X, each hold the full extent of parental responsibility as described at s 61C of the FLA.
His Honour Justice Jarrett concisely summarised approaches to the difficulty with s 61E in Haskova & Viraj [2025] FedCFamC1F 103, in relation to adoption proceedings in Queensland:
However, it is necessary to consider the effect of s 61E of the Act. Taken literally, the effect of this provision is to divest a non-adopting parent, including the parent who is the partner of the adopting party, of their parental responsibility for the adopted child. So much was explained in Cahill & Fryar [2008] FamCA 1245 at [13], Brock & Brock [2007] FamCA 1594 at [12], Tanner & Cape [2011] FamCA 665 at [15] and the other cases referred to therein.
However, in Hanlon & Hanlon [2022] FedCFamC1F 435, in what is, with respect, a careful and well-reasoned judgment, Gill J took a different view and concluded that on its proper construction, s 61E of the Act did not operate to strip parental responsibility from the parent who was partnered with the adopting step-parent. To take that interpretation requires the text of s 61E to be read down such that its operation is confined to the parental responsibility of a parent who is not a partner of the adopting step-parent.
The perceived difficulty with s 61E has been dealt with in the past by ensuring that the partner of the adoptive step-parent (ie the parent of the child to be adopted) is a party to the application under s 60G of the Act. Indeed, as Carew J points out in Thomson & Barnes [2022] FedCFamC1F 504 at [13], the definition of prescribed adopting parent in s 4(1) of the Act includes a parent of a child. In a similar vein, in Queensland, any application for adoption of a child by a step-parent must be made jointly with the step-parent’s spouse: s 204(3) of the Adoption Act. The effect of a final order for adoption is prescribed by s 214 of the Adoption Act.
...
The applicants in this proceeding seek both parenting orders and an order for leave to proceed with an application for adoption in Victoria under the Adoption Act. As the mother and the stepfather are both applicants in this proceeding and likely to be in the Victorian proceedings for adoption, the issue is not problematic.
In Fogwell & Ashton (1993) FamCA 113; (1993) FLC 92-429, Justice Chisholm identified, in reference to s 60AA as it then was (and is now s 60G):
The correct approach is that the Court should grant leave in circumstances such as the present if it is satisfied that there is a real possibility that an adoption order would be made if the parties were to apply for such an order and that there are no circumstances which would lead the Court to doubt that the adoption application to be made would be likely to promote the child’s welfare.
I am satisfied that the applicants are likely able to meet the mandatory legislative requirements under the Adoption Act to obtain an adoption order if they apply for such an order for the following reasons:
(1) Pursuant to s 10(1)(a) of the Adoption Act, X, aged 10, is unlikely to attain the age of 18 years before an application is brought for her adoption.

(2) Pursuant to s 10A, the applicants may bring an application for an adoption order under ss 11 and 12 as they have been married for over two years, having married on in late 2022.

(3) Pursuant to s 11(5), the stepfather, as the husband of X’s mother, may make an application for an adoption order for X, likely with the mother’s consent as co-applicant. If the stepfather makes such an application under s 11(5), s 11(6) provides that the County Court must be satisfied that:

(a) the making of a family law parenting order in relation to the guardianship or custody of X would not adequately provide for her welfare and interests; and

(b) exceptional circumstances exist which warrant the making of an adoption order; and

(c) an order for X’s adoption would make better provision for her welfare and interests than an order in relation to guardianship or custody.

(4) Ultimately it is likely that pursuant to s 15, an order for X’s adoption will be considered in her best interests.

As noted above, it is not mandatory under the Victorian Adoption Act for leave to be granted by this Court before an adoption order can be applied for. However, the applicants in this matter sought both parenting orders and leave. I am satisfied that the applicants can meet the legislative requirements to proceed with X’s adoption.
Finally, I am satisfied that granting leave for the adoption proceedings to commence is in X’s best interests under s 60G(2) of the Act, for the same reasons that I am satisfied that the parenting orders as sought by the applicants are in her best interests. X’s adoption by the stepfather will support her long-term stability, emotional wellbeing and existing familial relationships.

CONCLUSION

I find that the orders sought by the applicants are in X’s best interests. Such orders will ensure her future wellbeing as both applicants will continue to provide a stable, loving and nurturing environment for X and her siblings.
The respondent has been absent in X’s life. I am satisfied that the stepfather wishes to take the further step of applying to adopt X to acknowledge his parental role and discharge his parenting obligations towards her. He deposes to this being a privilege.
I am further satisfied that changing X’s name to align with that of her siblings and parents is in her best interests and will assist in recognising her family connection and membership.
The Court shall make the orders sought by the applicants accordingly.
